criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the linear regression setting, linear self-attention model definition (Equation 1), ICL risk formulation (Equation 2), and the block-diagonal parameter constraint (Equation 6).

The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the theoretical framework for task vector emergence, including the pairwise demonstration setup (Equation 7) and triplet demonstration setup (Equation 9).
The agent has read Section 4 to understand how task vector injection reconstructs a 1-shot prompt (Equation 10) and why task vectors are limited to rank-one mappings.

The agent has read Section 6.1 to check the synthetic experiment settings: d=4, P_x = P_w = N(0, I_d), training with AdamW, batch size 1000, l1 regularization, 40 models per setting, varying L in [1,2,3] and n in [5,30].

The agent has read Section 6.2 and Appendix B.2 to check the practical LLM experiment settings: 33 tasks in 5 categories, prompt template (Equation 13), n=10 for task vector extraction, 50 prompts per task, injection at the best-performing layer.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the bijection task failure results for Llama-7B with n=10, covering tasks across domains X→Y, Y→X, and X↔Y.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the TaskV-M enhancement results on Llama-13B across 5 task categories (Knowledge, Algorithmic, Translation, Linguistic, Bijection) with n=10.
The plan includes implementing the linear-attention model (Equation 1) with trainable parameters V_l and Q_l under block-diagonal structure (Equation 6).
The plan includes training linear-attention models on random linear regression instances with single-token, pairwise, and triplet demonstration formats.

The plan includes the synthetic experiment hyperparameters: d=4, Gaussian distributions N(0, I_d), AdamW optimizer, batch size 1000, l1 regularization, 40 random seeds per setting, L in [1,2,3], n in [5,30].
The plan includes building the bijection tasks experiment with practical LLMs (GPT-J, Pythia variants, Llama-7B, Llama-13B), including both standard ICL and task vector injection.
The plan includes the prompt template format (Equation 13): 'Example: {x_i} → {y_i}\n' concatenated for each demonstration, with the query missing the response.
The plan includes the TaskV-M multi-vector injection strategy: generating N+1 distinct task vectors from N+1 different ICL prompts and injecting into all N+1 arrow tokens of a few-shot prompt.
The plan includes the saliency map computation and visualization for Llama-7B, computing S(A_l) = sum_h |A_{l,h} * dL/dA_{l,h}| to observe embedding concatenation and task vector formation patterns.

The plan includes replicating the task vector mechanism in linear-attention models: extracting hidden states of the last arrow token after the first layer, normalizing, and injecting into zero-shot prompts.

The code for the linear-attention model (Equation 1) has been implemented, including the attention function Attn_{V,Q}(Z) = VZM(Z^T QZ) with masking matrix M = diag(I_{d_p-1}, 0) and skip connections.
The code for the ICL risk loss function (Equation 2) has been implemented as the expected squared norm between model output and -Wx_test over random linear regression instances.
The code for the input prompt format with single-token demonstrations (Equation 3) has been implemented, constructing Z_0 as a 2d x (n+1) matrix with X and Y interleaved.
The code for the input prompt format with pairwise demonstrations (Equation 7) has been implemented, constructing Z_0 as a 2d x (2n+2) matrix with separate x_i and y_i tokens.
The code for the input prompt format with triplet demonstrations (Equation 9) has been implemented, constructing Z_0 as a 2d x (3n+3) matrix with x_i, zero (arrow), and y_i as separate tokens.

The code for the attention layer with position encodings (Equation 5) has been implemented using one-hot position encodings P = I_{d_p} and the reformulated attention Attn_{V,Q}(Z) = VZM[Z^T P^T]Q[Z; P].
The code for the block-diagonal parameter structure (Equation 6) has been implemented: V_l = diag(A_l, B_l) and Q_l = diag(C_l, 0_{d x d}, D_l) for the linear-attention transformer.
The code for generating random linear regression instances has been implemented: sampling x_i ~ N(0, I_d), w_j ~ N(0, I_d), and computing y_i = Wx_i for the in-context training data.
The code for training linear-attention models with AdamW optimizer, batch size 1000, l1 regularization, and restricting A_l, B_l, C_l to S_I (scalar multiples of identity) has been implemented.
The code for training 40 models per configuration with different random seeds and reporting the minimum achieved ICL risk has been implemented.

The code for the task vector extraction in linear-attention models has been implemented: extracting the hidden state of the last arrow token after the first attention layer from triplet-formatted ICL prompts.
The code for task vector injection into zero-shot prompts in linear-attention models has been implemented: normalizing the task vector and injecting into zero-shot prompts consisting of x_test only.
The code for computing the evaluation risk for task vectors in linear-attention models as ||y_hat/||y_hat|| + y_test/||y_test||||_2 (to simulate layer normalization) has been implemented.
The code for loading and using practical LLMs (at minimum Llama-7B and Llama-13B) for ICL experiments has been implemented.

The code for constructing ICL prompts using the exact template (Equation 13) has been implemented: 'Example: {x_i} → {y_i}\n' for each demonstration, with the query token having the arrow but no response.
The code for the 33 ICL tasks across 5 categories (Knowledge, Algorithmic, Translation, Linguistic, Bijection) has been implemented with proper input-output pair generation as described in Table 5.

The code for the bijection tasks (combining a mapping with its inverse) has been implemented, including tasks such as 'To Upper & Lower', 'English & French', 'Present & Gerund', 'Singular & Plural', etc.

The code for task vector extraction from practical LLMs has been implemented: generating 50*(N+1) ICL prompts with n=10 demonstrations and recording hidden states of the last arrow token at every layer.
The code for task vector injection into practical LLMs has been implemented: replacing hidden states of arrow tokens at a specified layer l with the extracted task vectors.

The code for the multi-vector injection strategy (TaskV-M) has been implemented: generating N+1 distinct task vectors from N+1 different ICL prompts and injecting each into the corresponding arrow token position in a few-shot prompt.

The code for the evaluation protocol for practical LLM experiments has been implemented: measuring accuracy as whether the next predicted word matches the expected answer, averaged over 5 independent trials, with mean and standard deviation reported.
The code for the layer selection for task vector injection has been implemented: evaluating performance at each layer l in L and selecting the best-performing layer for reporting.

The code for saliency map computation has been implemented: computing S(A_l) = sum_h |A_{l,h} * dL/dA_{l,h}| where A_{l,h} is the attention matrix of the h-th head at layer l and L is the ICL cross-entropy loss.

The code for the single-prompt multi-vector variant (TaskV-MS) has been implemented: extracting multiple task vectors from each arrow token in a single few-shot prompt and injecting them simultaneously.

The code for the D_l weight visualization has been implemented to produce heatmaps showing the block-diagonal structure for pairwise demonstrations (aligned with Theorem 1) and the richer structure for triplet demonstrations (aligned with Theorem 2).
The synthetic linear-attention model training experiments across single-token, pairwise, and triplet formats with L in [1,2,3] and n in [5,30] have been executed successfully without errors.

The task vector mechanism reproduction in linear-attention models has been executed successfully: extracting task vectors from triplet-formatted ICL prompts, normalizing, injecting into zero-shot prompts, and computing the final ICL risk.

The bijection task experiments on practical LLMs (at minimum Llama-7B with n=10) comparing many-shot ICL and task vector injection across all bijection tasks and their X→Y, Y→X, X↔Y splits have been executed successfully.
The TaskV-M enhancement experiments on Llama-13B across all 5 task categories with 0-shot through 4-shot settings (Baseline, TaskV, TaskV-M) with 5 independent trials have been executed successfully.
The bijection task experiments across all 7 LLMs (GPT-J, Pythia-6.9B, Pythia-12B, Llama-7B, Llama-13B, Qwen3-8B, Llama3-8B) with n=10 and n=20 have been executed successfully.
The saliency map experiments on Llama-7B with n=10 demonstrations have been executed successfully to produce bipartite graph visualizations of information flow.
The execution outcomes for Figures 4a and 4b match the paper: pairwise and triplet formatted models achieve similar ICL risk, both falling between L-layer and (L-1)-layer single-token baselines.
The execution outcomes for Figure 4c match the paper: the performance of task vectors in linear-attention models is highly correlated with standard 1-shot ICL risk, validating the conjecture.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: on bijection tasks (X↔Y), task vector accuracy is near or below 60% (near-random 50%) while many-shot ICL maintains substantially higher accuracy on Llama-7B with n=10.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: on non-bijection tasks (X→Y and Y→X), both ICL and task vectors perform well (above 60%), while bijection tasks show consistent task vector failure.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: Copy and Antonym are exceptions where task vectors do not fail on bijection tasks (consistent with Proposition 4's special cases x=y and x=-y).
The execution outcomes match Table 2: TaskV-M consistently outperforms TaskV across all task categories and shot counts (1-shot through 4-shot) on Llama-13B, especially on bijection tasks.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: TaskV outperforms Baseline (standard few-shot ICL) across Knowledge, Algorithmic, Translation, and Linguistic tasks on Llama-13B.

The execution outcomes match Tables 8 and 9: task vector failure on bijection tasks is consistent across multiple LLM architectures (GPT-J, Pythia-6.9B, Pythia-12B, Llama-7B, Llama-13B, Qwen3-8B, Llama3-8B) with both n=10 and n=20 demonstrations.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3 (saliency maps): at an earlier layer (l=10), y_i tokens primarily attend to their corresponding (x_i, y_i) pair, reflecting the embedding concatenation mechanism.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3 (saliency maps): at a later layer (l=12, just before the optimal injection layer l=13), the final arrow token attends broadly to all y_i tokens, reflecting the task vector formation mechanism.
The execution outcomes match Figure 2: the learned D_l matrices for pairwise demonstrations exhibit a block-diagonal structure aligned with the S_P set in Theorem 1.
The execution outcomes match Figure 2: the learned D_l matrices for triplet demonstrations exhibit a richer, non-block-diagonal structure aligned with the more complex S_P set in Theorem 2.
The execution outcomes match Figure 2c: the learned Lambda_4 matrix has nearly orthonormal rows (Lambda_4 * Lambda_4^T is approximately proportional to diag(I_n, 0)), consistent with Proposition 3.
The execution outcomes match Table 6: TaskV-M consistently improves over TaskV on Pythia-12B across all task categories, demonstrating generalization of the multi-vector method beyond Llama-13B.
The execution outcomes match Table 7: TaskV-MS (single-prompt multi-vector variant) achieves comparable but slightly lower performance than TaskV-M (multi-prompt variant) on Llama-13B.
